4 min read
Just now

--

Everyone selling the AI transformation talks about what it can replace. The person running the infrastructure behind most of it just said, in public, what it shouldn’t.
A room full of executives tells Amazon Web Services CEO Matt Garman their plan: use AI to replace their junior hires entirely. According to accounts of the exchange that later circulated widely, Garman didn’t hedge. He called it one of the dumbest things he’d ever heard, and he’s kept saying it since most recently reiterating the point in an interview with WIRED, at the same time confirming Amazon plans to bring in 11,000 interns and new graduates this year.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ai Generated ImageMost tech leaders pitching AI transformation frame it around what the technology can take off a company’s hands. The person whose company sells the compute underneath most of that transformation is the one publicly drawing a line around what shouldn’t be handed over.
The Argument, In Plain Terms
Garman’s reasoning boils down to a few concrete points, not sentiment:

• Junior employees are usually the cheapest people on payroll. Cutting them first to save cost, rather than cutting anywhere else, doesn’t actually optimize for savings it optimizes for appearances.
